coredata.dc:title: Hybrid of quantum dots for interfacial tension reduction and reservoir alteration wettability for enhanced oil recovery (EOR)
coredata.dc:creator: AfzaliTabar, Mahsan
Rashidi, Alimorad
Alaei, Mahshad
Koolivand, Hadis
Pourhashem, Sepideh
Askari, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: Nanoparticle stabilized emulsions in enhanced oil recovery are more attractive and practical than conventional emulsions which stabilized by colloidal particles and different surfactants due to their advantages and special characteristics such as high stability in harsh condition, move long distance in reservoirs without high retention due to small size of nanoparticles. Only one third of original oil in place (OIP) is usually produced and two third of oil in place will be trapped to reservoir rockthus suitable chemical enhanced oil recovery (C-EOR) methods should be used. In this research, we have suggested a novel, economical and commercial method for synthesis N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/MoS2 quantum dots (MQDs) nanohybrids for preparing different percentage of Nanoemulsions which can reduce alterfacial tension significantly so it can used for Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) application. MoO3 material was used as a base of MQDs. MQDs was synthesized via exfoliation of MoS2 nanoparticles by Butyl lithium under N2 atmosphere condition. N-GQDs were synthesized by citric acid and urea materials via hydrothermal method. GQDs/MQDs were prepared via a simple sol-gel method for 5 h string. Synthetic materials were characterized with X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–visible absorption, Scanning Microscopic Electron (SEM), EDX profile and mapping, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Then, emulsions were prepared with two different cationic and anionic surfactants and the stability and morphology of emulsion droplets were investigated in condition close to reservoir environment. Our results show that 10% GQDs/MQDs in cationic surfactant and 50% GQDs/MQDs in anionic surfactant have good stability and very small and fine emulsion droplets in simulated reservoir conditions in laboratory. The Interfacial Tension (IFT) measurement shows >70% improvement which indicates the high ability of these nanohybrids in reducing the surface tension than previous nanohybrids. Contact angle values show that these nanohybrids can alter the wettability of reservoir rock from oil-wet to water-wet so the trapped oil in the porous region of rock can be easily extracted in the presence of a layer of these nanohybrids. Furthermore, according to the results of altering the density and viscosity of nanohybrids, these are not as limiting parameters and only about 1% increasing observed for density and viscosity, respectively. Coreflooding test revealed the high oil recovery efficiency (22%) at very low nanofluid concentration (0.01 wt%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enhanced oil recovery (EOR)
Quantum dot hybrids
Nanohybrids emulsion
Graphene quantum dots
Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots
Rock reservoir alteration wettability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MoS2 nanoparticles and MoS2 quantum dots
Preparation of nanoemulsions with SDBS and CTAB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of a) MQDs and b) N-GQDs; droplet size distribution of c)MQDs and d)N-GQDs; HRTEM images of e and f) MQDs, g and h)N-GQDs.
XRD pattern of a)MoS2 nanoparticle b)MQDs c) N-GQDs, d) 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid and e) 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid.
The FT-IR spectra of the N-GQDs (a), 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid(b) and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid.
SEM image of 10% nanohybrid N-GQDs/MQDs (a), EDX profile of 10% nanohybrid N-GQDs/MQDs (b) and elemental mapping with different colors.
SEM image of 50% nanohybrid N-GQDs/MQDs (a), EDX profile of 50% nanohybrid N-GQDs/MQDs (b) and elemental mapping with different colors.
Emulsion stability of nanohybrids with two different cationic and anionic surfactants over a) 24 h, b) 2 weeks and c) after a month.
Microscopic images of Nanoemulsions over 24 h, 2 weeks, and a month.
Stability of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs emulsions at different salinity.
Stability of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanoemulsions at alkaline pH.
Stability of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs emulsions at different temperature.
Interfacial tension of a)water droplet b)50% N-GQDs/MQDs and c)10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids.
Contact angle value of a)kerosene droplet as a reference b)10% N-GQDs/MQDs and c)50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids.
Oil recovery for water flooding and nanofluid flooding for a)Core No.1 and b)Core No.2.
oil recovery of nanofluid flooding at two different concentration.

row.entry: Oil recovery to overall oil recovery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: comparison of the effect of nanohybrid prepared in this study with other researchers.

row.entry: Enhanced oil recovery factor after nanofluid flooding (%)

head.ce:title: Hybrid of quantum dots for interfacial tension reduction and reservoir alteration wettability for enhanced oil recovery (EOR)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoparticle stabilized emulsions in enhanced oil recovery are more attractive and practical than conventional emulsions which stabilized by colloidal particles and different surfactants due to their advantages and special characteristics such as high stability in harsh condition, move long distance in reservoirs without high retention due to small size of nanoparticles. Only one third of original oil in place (OIP) is usually produced and two third of oil in place will be trapped to reservoir rockthus suitable chemical enhanced oil recovery (C-EOR) methods should be used. In this research, we have suggested a novel, economical and commercial method for synthesis N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/MoS2 quantum dots (MQDs) nanohybrids for preparing different percentage of Nanoemulsions which can reduce alterfacial tension significantly so it can used for Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) application. MoO3 material was used as a base of MQDs. MQDs was synthesized via exfoliation of MoS2 nanoparticles by Butyl lithium under N2 atmosphere condition. N-GQDs were synthesized by citric acid and urea materials via hydrothermal method. GQDs/MQDs were prepared via a simple sol-gel method for 5 h string. Synthetic materials were characterized with X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–visible absorption, Scanning Microscopic Electron (SEM), EDX profile and mapping, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Then, emulsions were prepared with two different cationic and anionic surfactants and the stability and morphology of emulsion droplets were investigated in condition close to reservoir environment. Our results show that 10% GQDs/MQDs in cationic surfactant and 50% GQDs/MQDs in anionic surfactant have good stability and very small and fine emulsion droplets in simulated reservoir conditions in laboratory. The Interfacial Tension (IFT) measurement shows >70% improvement which indicates the high ability of these nanohybrids in reducing the surface tension than previous nanohybrids. Contact angle values show that these nanohybrids can alter the wettability of reservoir rock from oil-wet to water-wet so the trapped oil in the porous region of rock can be easily extracted in the presence of a layer of these nanohybrids. Furthermore, according to the results of altering the density and viscosity of nanohybrids, these are not as limiting parameters and only about 1% increasing observed for density and viscosity, respectively. Coreflooding test revealed the high oil recovery efficiency (22%) at very low nanofluid concentration (0.01 wt%).

ce:section.ce:para: Oil recovery from reservoirs with traditional methods can only recover two-thirds of the oil approximately [1]. So to recover entrapped oil of reservoirs, suitable EOR methods are required [2]. Microemulsion flooding containing nanoparticles is more attractive than polymer, alkaline, and surfactant flooding in recent years due to its outstanding features, including low price, more environmentally friendly, and more efficiency [1]. They can improve oil recovery by changing the interfacial tension, wettability, contact angle, viscosity, etc. [3–8]. Sometimes Polymer flooding can create a problem in low permeability reservoirs due to have molecular weight of 10–25 million Daltons [9]. Flooding process assisted with surfactants in reservoirs is delicate to hydrolysis, and the effectiveness of these flooding may be decreased in high salinity and high temperature [10]. The pores size of Reservoirs is generally in micrometer diameters; so nanoparticles can move through them easily [11].

Many researchers are interested in studying Nanoemulsions for different targets, especially for EOR process [12,13]. EOR can be increased by increasing the size of nanoparticles, temperature, nanofluid injection flow and nanofluid concentration to critical value [14,15].

Henderaningrat et al. [16–18] studied the influence of silica nanofluid on the wettability of rock reservoirs in different situations such as different concentrations and size of silica nanoparticles, temperature, and core wettability. Giraldo et al. [19] used alumina nanofluid for enhanced oil recovery process. They found that alumina nanofluid could improve oil recovery by alteration the wettability of sandstone rocks.

Radnia et al. [20] studied the effect of sulfonated graphene on sandstone reservoirs. They found that sulfonated graphene nanofluid could change the wettability of sandstone rock from oil-wet to water-wet. Ehtesabi et al. [1] used TiO2 nanofluid for heavy oil recovery from sandstone reservoirs. They investigated anatase and amorphous structures of TiO2 and found that oil recovery can be improved from 49% to 80% by using anatase TiO2 nanoparticles. They also found that oil recovery for both TiO2 structures (anatase and amorphous), were decreased when the concentration of TiO2 nanoparticles were about 1%.

MoS2 categorize in transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDS), and it is composed of two dimensional S-Mo-S layers [21]. MoS2 quantum dots solution has many good advantages such as low toxicity and good dispersion [22]. MoS2 nanoparticles can be used as a nanocatalyst for oil recovery [23]. To prevent the aggregation of nanoparticles in nanofluids, they often used with different supports such as carbon structures [24–26].

Carbon has different forms. One of these forms is Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which have two or more graphene layers. The size of GQDs is <30 nm, and the lateral size of them is 100 nm. [27–29]. GQDs consist grafting conjugated π- π network [29]. They have a large diameter, high surface area, high quantum yield, and water solubility [30,31]. Hetero atom can be doped to GQDs, so this network can regulate the surface properties by adjusting the charge distribution in surface [32].

It should be noticed that no evaluation has been done about preparation of nanohybrids with source of quantum dot materials via simple, economical and commercial method and investigate their application for alteration of reservoir rock wettability from oil-wet to water-wet for extracting trapped oil from rock till now. So in this study, we synthesis N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids from N doped Graphene quantum dots and MoS2 quantum dots and investigate the effect of different parameters such as alteration of N-GQDs and MQDs concentration, pH, temperature and salinity in emulsion stability for enhanced oil recovery. Furthermore, the performance of emulsions was investigated in the vicinity of different cationic and anionic surfactants. Results indicated that 10% N-GQDs/MQDs-CTAB and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs-SDBS can produce stable emulsions with very uniform and small droplets which can decrease the IFT value and increase the hydrophilicity of the reservoir rock surface (water-wet). Coreflooding test revealed the high oil recovery efficiency (22%) at very low nanofluid concentration which makes them a good candidate for EOR application.

Citric acid monohydrate, urea, Molybdenum(VI) oxide, n-Butyllithium 15% solution in hexane, HCl 37%, Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), n-decane and 2-propanol provided from Merck company and Dodecyl benzene sulfonic acid, sodium salt 88% (SDBS) provided from Acros company without any impurity.

XRD pattern of samples performed by using a Holand Philips x-ray diffraction (Cu Ka, K = 1.5406°A) with suitable amount of powder and 4 °C.min−1 step size. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy obtained in the range of 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 using a Tensor 27, BrukerInc., Germany. For FTIR sample preparation, take a small amount of powder sample for mixing with KBr powder. Subsequently, grind the mixture for 3–5 min, press the powder for 2 min to form a pallet for analysis. UV–visible spectra were performed using a PhotonixAr spectrophotometer with suitable amount of liquid sample. Optical microscopic images provided with Quantimet-570 microscope. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images of MQDs and N-GQDs were performed by using Ziess E900 instrument. For TEM sample preparation, suitable amount of liquid solution was put in ultrasonic bath, after that add small drop of solution on TEM grid, then left the grid to air dry for TEM analysis. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images of MQDs and N-GQDs were performed by using Tecnai G2 F20 instrument at 200KV.

EDX spectra and MAP analysis were done by using FESEM instrument MIRA model with SMAX Detector. For FESEM sample preparation, a suitable amount of liquid was spread over a substrate, then waiting for solvent evaporation to use them for FESEM analysis. Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) analysis was performed using Diamond instrument with heating rate 10 °C.min−1 and nitrogen purging gas.

N-GQDs were synthesized via the proposed method of Qu et al. [33]. Briefly, 4 g citric acid and 3.5 g urea were added to 100 ml DI water. The solution was transferred to autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. Then the product was dried at 80 °C oven, and finally, N-GQDs powder was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MoS2 nanoparticles and MoS2 quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: MoS2 nanoparticles were synthesized in a tubular furnace by using Molybdenum trioxide nanoparticles as the primary source and H2S gas as sulfidizing agent, in the presence of He/H2 as reducing gas at 800 °C for 2 h [34].

For preparing MoS2 quantum dots, 1–3 g MoS2 nanoparticles were poured into the three-neck round bottom flask. 300–600 ml Butyllithium was added to balloon drop by drop while the system was under a nitrogen atmosphere. After 48 h string, water was added to the solution, and after 1 day string, HCl 15% added the solution drop wisely until the pH of solution decreased. Finally, the solution was filtered with hexagonal and DI water for several times; therefore, the black sediments and yellow solution of MoS2 quantum dots were separated from each other completely.

N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids were prepared via a simple sol-gel method. An appropriate amount of MQDs was added to N-GQDs for preparing 10 wt%, 50 wt%, and 70 wt% nanohybrids drop by drop at 80 °C and string for about 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanoemulsions with SDBS and CTAB
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing Nanoemulsions of 10 wt%, 50 wt%, and 70 wt% nanohybrids, 0.3 wt% SDBS/CTAB, 5.3 wt% n-decane as an oil model, and 5.3 wt% 2-propanol were added to 50 ml nanohybrids. Finally, the solution was sonicated for 10–15 min.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images of MQDs and N-GQDs and droplet size distribution were shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM and HRTEM images show that average droplet size of MQDs and N-GQDs (which determine with digimizer image analysis software by TEM images) were 2–5 nm and dispersed uniformly in solution without any agglomeration.

XRD pattern of MoS2 nanoparticles, MQDs, N-GQDs, 10% N-GQDs/MQDs, and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs were shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of MoS2 reveals the reflection peaks of hexagonal MoS2 (JCPDS#77–1716). No other phases can be indicated in the XRD pattern of MoS2 nanoparticles. The sharp peak of (002) in this sample is due to agglomeration of MoS2 nanosheets due to their high surface energy and high tendency for agglomeration. The crystallize size of MoS2 nanosheets is calculated based on Debye-Scherrer equation [35]:
D = 0.9
λ / β
cosθ
where β is the full width at half maximum in radians, λ is the wavelength of CuKα (1.5444 Å), θ is Bragg's angle and D is the crystallite size. Based on this equation, the crystallite size of MoS2 nanosheets is about 20 nm.

The XRD pattern of MQDs shows weak signals which due to ultrafine and high exfoliated number of MoS2 nanosheet and it seems that MoS2 quantum dots have the amorphous structure [33,34]. N-GQDs represents a broad peak at 2θ = 29° assigned to (002) diffraction peak of graphite with d-spacing of about 0.31 nm, and the broadness of this peak is due to the small particle size of CQDs [36,37]. This broad peak of N-GQDs with low intensity is due to few layer of graphene dots [38]. The XRD pattern of hybrid 10% N-GQDs/MQDs is quite similar to MQDs which can be assigned to small weight percentage of N-GQDs in this sample and therefore, the N-GQDs has insignificant influence on MQDs structure. However, by increasing the amount of N-MQDs in hybrids, i.e., hybrid 50%N-GQDs/MQDs, the broad characteristic peak of N-GQDs appears in the XRD pattern and the effect of N-GQDs on the structure of MQDs enhances notably.

Optical properties of prepared samples were investigated by UV–Visible spectroscopy shown in Fig. S1. The absorption spectrum of MQDs is shown in Fig. S(1a). One spectrum is observed in the near-ultraviolet (N-UV) in the region λ <300 nm. This spectrum was related to the excitonic feature of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots [39,40]. UV–Visible absorption of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs hybrids are shown in Fig. S(1b) and Fig. S(1c), respectively. A very defined peak at 337 nm illustrates the uniform size of SP2 carbon atoms that they are doped in SP3 matrix of carbon. This peak can observe due to n → π * transition [41,42].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are used to characterize the formation of hybrids between N-GQDs and MQDs. Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs, hybrid 10%, and hybrid 50%. The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs shows two broad bands at 3411 and 3199 cm−1, indicating the presence of OH and NH groups, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at 1574, 1340, and 1328 cm−1 are related to CC, CO, and CN bands, which represent the presence of the basic carbon component in the structure [43–45]. In the case of hybrid samples, a broad absorption band at about 3410–3430 cm−1 is observed which can be assigned to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups and intercalated water. Also, characteristic absorption bands of N-GQDs are found in the hybrid samples. The band appears at 1115 cm−1 for both hybrids is the characteristic peak of SO (sulfide oxide) asymmetric stretching. The peaks of molybdenum disulfide (MoS) are observed at 1011 and 485 cm−1 for hybrid 10% and 638 cm−1 for hybrid 50% [46–48]. Also, FTIR spectra show that the OH bands for hybrid 10% is sharper than the other two samples, which can be regarded to higher amount of MoS2 in sample. However, the N-GQDs have broader OH peak and therefore, the hybrid 50% sample with higher quantity of GQDs has broader OH band compared to hybrid 10%.

SEM, EDX profile and elemental mapping of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids are shown in Figs. 4 and 5
, respectively. To analyze the composition of nanohybrids, EDX images and elemental mapping of nanohybrids are recorded, and the elements are shown with different colors. Compared with EDX spectrum of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs, 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids showed decreased in Mo (from 46.35 to 30.15) and S (from 33.29 to 16.05) elements confirming the existence of more MoS2 in 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid. The EDS map, shows that the elements have distributed uniformely in the synthesized hybrid samples. However, the elemental quantity analysis provided in Figs. 4b and 5b completely indicate that the percent of carbon has increased by adding higher amounts of carbon dots, providing the hybrids are formed completely.

Thermal stability of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids were studied by Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) analysis at high temperature in N2 atmosphere and heating rate of 10 °C/min to investigate the resistance of nanohybrids at the highest reservoir temperature. DSC analysis can show heat flow due to the decomposition of nanohybrids. As can be seen in Fig. S2, a small amount of trapped water in both nanohybrids was lost from samples at about 100 °C, after that there is no endothermic and exothermic peak to exhibit the decomposition of nanohybrids up to 300 °C, so these nanohybrids can maintain their stability at high reservoir temperature. MoS2 show two major weight changes at two high temperatures which is not matter about these nanohybrids which use in oil recovery [49]. At 540 °C, MoS2 oxidized to MoO3 followed by sublimation of the oxide at 700 °C. Carbon structures such as graphite oxidation took place rapidly at 700 °C leading CO and CO2 gases [50].

Comparison between emulsions stability were investigated for 24 h, 2 weeks and a month were shown in Fig. 6 . As can be seen, 50% N-GQDs/MQDs-SDBS and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs-CTAB nanohybrids emulsions have good stability during a month without any precipitation. Other samples could not maintain stability over time in environmental conditions.

Optical microscopic images of Nanoemulsions are illustrated in Fig. 7 . As can be seen, the average diameter of emulsion droplets with SDBS surfactant is smaller than CTAB surfactant except for 10% N-GQDs/MQDs-CTAB. Based on DerjanguinVerway, Landau and Overbeek (DLVO) theory [51], the stability of nanoparticles in fluid depends on total electrical double layer repulsive forces between nanoparticles and van der Waals attractive forces inside the fluid based on the Brownian motion. The particles tend to agglomerate when the attractive forces are higher than repulsive forces; therefore, the suspension becomes unstable, and sedimentation occurs as a result of particles agglomeration. Therefore, formation of surfactant/nanoparticles aggregates is controlled by balancing of electrostatic repulsion, hydrophobic interactions, and van der Waals attractions. It is thus feasible that the surface charge density of nanohybrids modulates SDBS and CTAB adsorption.

Surface electric charges are regarded as the major parameter for the kinetic stability of nanofluid. The zeta potential of GQDs, MQDs, 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid were performed at various pH in Fig. S3. The functional groups such as phenol, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups contribute to their negative zeta potentials of N-GQDs. At higher pH, deporotonation of functional groups occurs which leads to reduce zeta potential but with reducing pH, zeta potential increased due to protonation of functional groups. For MQDs, at higher pH, SH− was deporotonated into S2− and S2−…Mo4+….OH– was formed simultaneously but by decreasing pH, colloidal particle of SH−…Mo4+…SH− was formed [52]. As expected, the zeta potential of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid is higher than 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid due to the higher GQDs content.

It has demonstrated that nanohybrids in aqueous dispersion net surface charge are negative owing to the hydroxyl group proton dissociation and negative charge of sulfur atoms. The hydrophobic interactions of SBDS and nanohybrids have the principal role in the adsorption process of SBDS. Further studies have shown that the hydrophilic head of surfactant is closer to the negative charge of nanoparticles. The mechanism that causes this to happen is not yet clear. The first guess is the existence of a dual-layer around the nanoparticles in which the nanoparticles with negative charge hold positive sodium ions around, and these ions absorb the negative head of the surfactant [53,54].

When the anionic surfactants such as SDBS are adsorbed onto the electrophilic surface of the graphene according to the theory of adsorption isotherms, electrostatic stabilization occurs, and an electrical double layer will be created as a result of adsorption; therefore, Columbic repulsion forces will be developed between graphene nanosheets. When the concentration of surfactant increases, the coverage of surfactant on the graphene nanosheets enhances, leading to increase of inner Helmholtz layer potential and improving the stability of suspension [55].

In the case of emulsions loaded with SDBS surfactant, 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids is the most optimal structure and has appropriate values of N-GQDs and MQDs structures and the emulsion has very small and uniform size of droplets even after a month.

Among CTAB emulsions, 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid has very small and uniform emulsion droplets even after a month. As expected, the addition of excess CTAB to the nanohybrids solution makes it unstable and, as shown in Fig. 7, CTAB causes flocculation of colloidal particles. The main reason for this phenomenon is the presence of electrostatic attraction forces between the hydrophilic surfactant head and nanoparticles. Changes in effective charge on the nanohybrid surface indicate this, the higher the concentration of CTAB, the more surfactant absorbed on the surface of the nanohybrid, which reduces the effective charge of nanohybrid. This behavior can be attributed to the role of cationic surfactant and occurrence of ligand wrapped reaction, so hydrophilic media of MQDs can be transferred to hydrophobic media [56].

Nanoemulsions that have uniform droplet size and same droplet size distribution are the most suitable and usually when the concentration of particles increases, the average droplet size of emulsions decreases [57–59].
The stability of selected Nanoemulsions was investigated at different salinity, pH and temperature.

The stability of Nanoemulsions of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids at different salinity of 0.1 wt%, 1 wt%, and 10 wt% shown in Fig. 8 . Both emulsions have good stability at different salinity, and no precipitation observed even after a month.

Optical microscopic images of nanoemulsions at different salinity of 0.1 wt%, 1 wt%, and 10 wt% were shown in Fig. S4. Both nanoemulsions have small and uniform droplets at 0.1 wt%, 1 wt% and 10 wt% salinity.

The stability of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanoemulsions at alkaline pH (pH = 9) illustrated in Fig. 9 . As this figure shows, both nanoemulsions have good stability in the alkaline environment without any precipitation. As the pH increases, fewer precense nanohybrid particles transfer to emulsion and water phase become more offended [60].

Optical microscopic images of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanoemulsions at alkaline environment shown in Fig. S5. As the figure illustrates, both emulsions have uniform and small droplets in alkaline pH.

Fig. 10 is revealed the stability of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanoemulsions at 90 °C and 120 °C to investigate the stability of nanohybrids at reservoir temperature. It can be seen that emulsions have good stability at both ambient and different reservoir temperatures.

Optical microscopic images of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanoemulsions at reservoir temperature are shown in Fig. S6. As can be seen, the emulsion droplets are fine and uniform at ambient temperature, but the size of the emulsion droplets increased slightly with increasing temperature. However, the emulsion droplets were still tine and uniform at high reservoir temperature. This may be due to more active Brownian motion at higher temperature which leads to an increase in emulsion droplet size [61].

Interfacial tension (IFT) results are shown in Fig. 11 . The value of IFT for a)water droplet b)50% N-GQDs/MQDs and c) 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid is 53.90mN/m, 21.69 mN/m and 15.72 mN/m respectively. Results show that 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid has the minimum amount of IFT so it can decrease IFT value better than 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid.
Each experiment was repeated at least three times to verify the reproducibility of the results and to eliminate the source of uncertainty.

The dynamic surface tension (ST) of pure MQDs and N-GQDs are presented in Fig. S7. The surface tension markedly dropped, however ST gradually approached to the equilibrium value after about 1500 s for MQDs and 2000 s for N-GQDs. Both nanofluids had lower values of equilibrium STs than pure water (72 mN/m). Some reasons of these reduction surface tensions are high surface activity of these nanofluids and migration of particles to air-water interface. Due to self-assemble tendency of particles at air/water interface, stable emulsion can form. Moreover, the adsorption-desorption process of nanoparticles are much slower than surfactant molecules due to their size of particles. Therefore, in the presence of systems which contain nanoparticles, the dynamic of adsorption at the air-water interface is slower than normal system and the values of starting point and equilibrium surface tension is not equivalent.

The contact angle values of the nanohybrids show in Fig. 12 . According to this figure, the contact angle between a) Kerosene droplet and carbonated rock b) Kerosene droplet and carbonated rock that coated with 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid and b) Kerosene droplet and carbonated rock that coated with 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid are 87.73°, 64.57° and 69.47°, respectively.
Each experiment was repeated at least three times to verify the reproducibility of the results and to eliminate the source of uncertainty.

This results show that 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid has lower value of contact angle than 50% N-GQDs/MQDs, and it has more hydrophilicity than other samples. So it can alter the wettability of carbonate reservoir rock from oil-wet to water-wet >50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrid.

As the solid nanoparticles have a higher density than fluids, dispersing a small amount of nanoparticles in a fluid can systematically increase the density of the mixture [56]. The density of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluids was examined as a function of temperature. The density of the fluid decreased by increasing of temperature, which can be explained by the liquid thermal expansion [62].

As can be seen in Fig. S8, 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid has a higher density than 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluids due to the higher mass of molybdenum. The maximum increment of the density of the 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluids was 1078.21 and 1050.54 kg/m3 at 20 °C, respectively. It is worth to mention that in all ranges of temperature, density increment was not noticeable; as a result, density cannot be considered as a limiting parameter.

The viscosity of prepared nanofluids has been investigated in this part as a function of temperature. The viscosity of nanofluids depends on various factors such as the methods used to stabilize the nanoparticles, particles size, particles loading, and base fluid characters [63].

Fig. S9 shows the viscosity measurement of 10% N-GQDs/MQDs and 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid as a function of temperature. The nanoparticles tend to accumulate by attractive forces (Van der Waals), but repulsive forces (steric stabilization) prevent the aggregation of particles. Increasing the temperature resulted in increasing the particles Brownian motions, weakening of the interparticle and molecular adhesion forces, and leading to the decrease in the fluid viscosity [64].

In overall, the viscosity of 50% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid was higher than that of the 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid at all temperature ranges; however, the maximum increase in viscosity is only about 1% in 20 °C which is negligible, and due to the slope of curve, this small amount has decreased constantly by increasing the temperature. It is important to note that the different shape of nanoparticles affect the viscosity of nanofluids. Nanofluids with platelet shape of nanoparticles have greater viscosity than cylinder, blade, brick and spherical shape of nanoparticle. Nevertheless, by increasing volume fraction of nanoparticles, viscosity of nanofluid increased [65]. Despite of this fact in water base nanofluid, synthesized nanofluid in this work show negligible increasing viscosity which makes them suitable for EOR application.

Furthermore, Maje Alhaji Haruna et al. [66] demonstrate the effect of CQDs in polyacrylamide (PAM) solution for EOR application. They found that a sharp decrease of effective viscosity was observed at 0.05 wt% CQDs concentration. This difference may due to the different effect of the nanoparticles in the different media of polymeric and water aqueous which have different rheology effect.

The coreflood experiments reveal the nanofluids application ability for the enhanced oil recovery in carbonated reservoir rock. The flooding experiments are conducted at room temperature. The water was injected at constant rate of 0.2 ml/min and the volume of effluents (oil and water) was recorded every 5 min. Water flooding process was continued till no more oil is produced. Then the nanofluid injection is continued at constant rate 0.2 ml/min. Nanoflooding is conducted at two different concentrations till no more oil is produced.

The efficiency of 10 wt% hybrid nanofluid for enhanced oil recovery was measured by coreflood experiment at room temperature. Properties of rock temperature are listed in Table 1 . Each measurement was repeated at least three times to verify the reproducibility of the results and to eliminate the source of uncertainity.

Water flooding as a secondary oil recovery was done till all movement oil is produced by water injection. After that, nanofluid injection at two different concentration of 0.01 wt% and 0.005 wt% was continued as tertiary oil recovery until no more oil is produced.

As it is shown in Fig. 13 (a) and (b), the oil recovery at the breakthrough was 62% and 44% for water flooding in the cores 1 and 2, respectively. Additional oil recovery that was observed from the injection of nanofluid at concentrations of 0.01 wt% and 0.005 wt% were 22% and 15%, respectively. It indicates the efficiency of the 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid in enhanced oil recovery. According to the results, 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanofluid exhibited an excellent capability that made oil molecules displace from an oil-wet surface.
The percent of oil recovery with injection of water and synthesis nanofluid are presented Table 2 .

According to coreflood experiment results, the percent of oil recovery with injection of nanofluid in core 1 is better than core 2 despite of better characterization (such as porosity and permeability) of core 2 than core 1. This observation is due to the high concentration of nanofluid injection in core 1. Furthermore, oil recovery percent in core 1 after water flooding is higher than core 2. This observation is due to the higher initial water saturation (SWi) in core 2 than core 1, in the other words it is clear that the core 1 is more water-wet that core 2.
The nanofluid behavior developed in this study in EOR application was compared with nanofluids used by other researchers, and the results are shown in Table 3 .

The result from coreflooding measurement showed that the oil enhancement efficiency of 10% MoS2 quantum dot/N-graphene quantum dot nanofluid injection is 22% (at very low permeability and porosity characterization of cores) which is higher than previous studies.

In this study, we prepared different percent of N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids via a facile and economical sol-gel method. Synthesized materials were characterized by XRD, FTIR, UV visible absorption, DSC and TEM. Nanoemulsions of nanohybrids prepared by using suitable anionic and cationic surfactant, n-Octane as an oil model and 2-propanol as co-surfactant in ambient temperature and neutral pH.

The stability of Nanoemulsions with different surfactants evaluated for a month. The morphology of Nanoemulsions investigated by optical microscopy. Results showed that 50% N-GQDs/MQDs and 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids have very good stability in both ambient conditions and at different pH, salinity, and temperature. IFT results showed that 10% N-GQDs/MQDs nanohybrids had a minimum amount of IFT value. The contact angle results indicate that this nanohybrid can change the wettability of reservoir rock from oil-wet to water-wet better than other nanohybrids; Coreflooding measurement showed that the oil enhancement efficiency of 10% MoS2 quantum dot/N-graphene quantum dot nanofluid injection is 22% at very low nanofluid concentration. Accordingly, it would be a suitable candidate for C-EOR application.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Mahsan AfzaliTabar: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Alimorad Rashidi: Conceptualization, Supervision. Mahshad Alaei: Investigation. Hadis Koolivand: Investigation. Sepideh Pourhashem: Investigation. Saeed Askari: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
